epigraph.text:
From one man he made all the nations, that they 
should inhabit the whole earth; and he marked out their appointed 
times in history and the boundaries of their lands.
epigraph.quote: Mathematics knows no races or geographic boundaries.

content.text: {Mathematics knows no races or geographic boundaries.}{David Hilbert}

In manufacturing, medicine, and engineering, we often care not just 
about whether a measurement is above or below a target, but about 
*how far* it is from the target. A bolt that is 0.02 cm too long 
or 0.02 cm too short might both be acceptable—what matters is the 
*distance* from the ideal length. This concept of distance from 
a central value is captured beautifully by **absolute value 
inequalities**.

Recall that the **absolute value** of a number is its distance 
from zero on the number line. Distance is always non-negative.

content.title: Absolute Value
content.content:
The **absolute value** of a real number $x$, 
written $|x|$, is defined as:
\[
 |x| = \begin{cases}
 x, & \text{if } x \geq 0 \\
 -x, & \text{if } x < 0
 \end{cases}
\]
Geometrically, $|x|$ represents the **distance** from $x$ to $0$ 
on the number line.

More generally, $|x - a|$ represents the distance from $x$ to $a$.

content.text:
When we say $|x| < 3$, we're asking: which numbers are *less than 
3 units away from zero*? Looking at the number line, these are all the 
numbers between $-3$ and $3$.

content.title: Absolute Value ``Less Than'' Inequalities
content.content:
For any positive number $c$:
\[
 |x| < c \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad -c < x < c
\]
\[
 |x| \leq c \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad -c \leq x \leq c
\]
In words: "The distance from $x$ to zero is less than $c$" means 
"$x$ is between $-c$ and $c$."
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Solving $|x| < 4$
Solving $|x - 2| \leq 5$
Solving $|2x + 1| < 7$
content.solution:
Apply the theorem:
\[
 |2x + 1| < 7 \implies -7 < 2x + 1 < 7
\]

Subtract 1 from all parts:
\[
 -7 - 1 < 2x < 7 - 1
\]
\[
 -8 < 2x < 6
\]

Divide all parts by 2:
\[
 -4 < x < 3
\]

Interval notation: $(-4, 3)$

content.text:
When we say $|x| > 3$, we're asking: which numbers are *more than 
3 units away from zero*? These are numbers less than $-3$ OR greater 
than $3$. There's no way to write this as a single interval—we need 
two separate pieces.

content.title: Absolute Value ``Greater Than'' Inequalities
content.content:
For any positive number $c$:
\[
 |x| > c \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad x < -c \;\text{ or }\; x > c
\]
\[
 |x| \geq c \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad x \leq -c \;\text{ or }\; x \geq c
\]
In words: "The distance from $x$ to zero is greater than $c$" means 
"$x$ is outside the interval from $-c$ to $c$."
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Solving $|x| > 2$
Solving $|x + 3| \geq 4$
Solving $|4x - 5| > 11$
content.solution:
Apply the theorem:
\[
 |4x - 5| > 11 \implies 4x - 5 < -11 \;\text{ or }\; 4x - 5 > 11
\]

Solve each inequality:
$$
 4x - 5 < -11 &\implies 4x < -6 \implies x < -\frac{3}{2} \\
 4x - 5 > 11 &\implies 4x > 16 \implies x > 4
$$

Solution: $x < -\frac{3}{2}$ or $x > 4$

Interval notation: $\left(-\infty, -\frac{3}{2}\right) \cup (4, \infty)$

content.text: Some absolute value inequalities have special solutions.

content.title: No solution
content.solution:
Absolute value is *always* non-negative. The smallest value $|x + 2|$ 
can be is 0. It can never be less than $-3$ (or any negative number).

Solution: **No solution** ($\emptyset$)

content.title: All real numbers
content.solution:
Absolute value is always non-negative, so $|x - 1| \geq 0$. Since 
$0 > -5$, the inequality $|x - 1| > -5$ is *always true*.

Solution: **All real numbers** $(-\infty, \infty)$

content.title: Exactly one solution
content.solution:
Since $|x - 4| \geq 0$ always, the only way for $|x - 4| \leq 0$ to be 
true is if $|x - 4| = 0$.

This happens when $x - 4 = 0$, so $x = 4$.

Solution: $x = 4$ (a single point)

content.title: Recognizing Special Cases
content.content:
Before solving an absolute value inequality:

- If $|expression| < $ (negative number), there is **no solution**.

- If $|expression| >$ (negative number), the solution is 
 **all real numbers**.

- If $|expression| \leq 0$, the solution is where the expression 
 equals zero.

- If $|expression| \geq 0$, the solution is **all real numbers**.

content.text:
One of the most common applications of absolute value inequalities is 
in describing **tolerance**—the acceptable deviation from an 
ideal value.

content.title: Tolerance
content.content:
In manufacturing and engineering, **tolerance** specifies how much 
a measurement can differ from the ideal value. If a measurement $x$ must 
be within $t$ units of an ideal value $c$, this is written as:
\[
 |x - c| \leq t
\]

content.title: Manufacturing tolerance
content.problem:
A machine part must be 25.0 mm long with a tolerance of $\pm 0.2$ mm. 
Write and solve an inequality describing acceptable lengths.
content.solution:
The length $L$ must be within 0.2 mm of 25.0 mm:
\[
 |L - 25.0| \leq 0.2
\]

Solving:
\[
 -0.2 \leq L - 25.0 \leq 0.2
\]
\[
 24.8 \leq L \leq 25.2
\]

Acceptable lengths are between **24.8 mm and 25.2 mm**, inclusive.

content.title: Temperature control
content.problem:
A greenhouse thermostat is set to 72°F with a sensitivity of 3°F. This 
means the heating turns on when the temperature drops more than 3°F 
below 72°F, and cooling turns on when it rises more than 3°F above 72°F. 
When is neither heating nor cooling active?
content.solution:
Neither system is active when the temperature $T$ is within 3°F of 72°F:
\[
 |T - 72| \leq 3
\]

Solving:
\[
 -3 \leq T - 72 \leq 3
\]
\[
 69 \leq T \leq 75
\]

Neither heating nor cooling is active when the temperature is between 
**69°F and 75°F**.

content.title: Quality control
content.problem:
A beverage company fills bottles with a target of 16.9 fluid ounces. 
Bottles with more than 0.3 oz deviation from target are rejected. 
Write an inequality for rejected bottles.
content.solution:
Let $V$ = volume in fluid ounces. Bottles are rejected when the deviation 
exceeds 0.3 oz:
\[
 |V - 16.9| > 0.3
\]

Solving:
\[
 V - 16.9 < -0.3 \quad\text{or}\quad V - 16.9 > 0.3
\]
\[
 V < 16.6 \quad\text{or}\quad V > 17.2
\]

Bottles are rejected if the volume is less than **16.6 oz** or 
greater than **17.2 oz**.

content.title: The Origins of Precision Manufacturing
content.content:
The concept of manufacturing tolerance emerged during the Industrial 
Revolution when Eli Whitney demonstrated interchangeable parts for 
muskets in 1801. Before this, each part was hand-fitted. The mathematical 
language of tolerances—essentially absolute value inequalities—became 
essential for mass production. Today, computer chip manufacturing 
achieves tolerances measured in nanometers, requiring extraordinary 
precision in both machinery and mathematics.

marginNotes.text:
Where can I go from your Spirit? 
Where can I flee from your presence? If I go up to the heavens, you 
are there; if I make my bed in the depths, you are there.

Can you fathom the mysteries of God? Can 
you probe the limits of the Almighty? They are higher than the heavens 
above—what can you do? They are deeper than the depths below—what 
can you know?

A false balance is an abomination to 
the Lord, but a just weight is his delight.

marginNotes.author: Johannes Kepler
marginNotes.text:
Geometry is one and eternal shining in 
the mind of God.

marginNotes.author: G.H. Hardy
marginNotes.text:
A mathematician, like a painter or poet, is 
a maker of patterns.
